title: Monitoring Athlete Training Loads: Consensus Statement
notes: Expert framework supports separate internal and external load taxonomy; no youth middle-distance validation or universal total.

title: Internal and External Training Load: 15 Years On
notes: Conceptual two-component framework; supports family identity but is not a validation study.

title: A new approach to monitoring exercise training
training_status: Exercise participants; exact composition not verified
notes: Session-RPE related consistently to HR-based scores but absolute values differed; methods are not numerically interchangeable.

title: The Validation of Session Rating of Perceived Exertion for Quantifying Internal Training Load in Adolescent Distance Runners
sex: BOTH; exact counts not reported in abstract
notes: Direct timing evidence: 30-minute sRPE was 26.5% lower than immediate sRPE; timing is part of method identity.

title: Session-RPE Method for Training Load Monitoring: Validity, Ecological Usefulness, and Influencing Factors
population: Athletes across sex age level and sport
notes: Review identified 36 modified-CR10 validation and reliability studies; heterogeneity limits direct youth runner transfer.

title: Comparison of RPE (Rating of Perceived Exertion) Scales for Session RPE
population: Recreational systematically training athletes across sports
notes: CR10 and Borg 6-20 sRPE correlated r=.90 but produced different absolute values; scale ID and anchors must persist.

title: 25 Years of Session Rating of Perceived Exertion: Historical Perspective and Development
population: Patients through elite athletes across exercise modes
notes: Defines sRPE as an entire-session average internal response; does not validate allocation to session components.

title: Relation between individualized training impulses and performance in distance runners
notes: Individualized HR-lactate TRIMPi related to outcomes when group-average weighting did not; method identity and individual thresholds are required.

title: Do Training Load Metrics Agree? A Comparison of Session Rate of Perceived Exertion, Physiological and Biomechanical Load in Outdoor Running
notes: sRPE TRIMP and biomechanical load correlations were weak to moderate across three session types; separate measures must not be summed as one currency.

title: Concurrent validity and reliability of the session rating of perceived exertion scale among high-trained rower during training sessions
notes: sRPE-TRIMP correlation varied from .811 in ergometer work to .258 in functional training; validity is modality-specific.

title: Investigating sources of inaccuracy in wearable optical heart rate sensors
notes: Wearable HR error differed by device and activity and was about 30% higher during activity than rest; model and protocol provenance are required.

title: Accuracy in Wrist-Worn, Sensor-Based Measurements of Heart Rate and Energy Expenditure in a Diverse Cohort
notes: Most devices measured some HR conditions adequately but no device estimated energy expenditure below 20% error; outcomes are non-equivalent.

title: Accuracy of Distance Recordings in Eight Positioning-Enabled Sport Watches: Instrument Validation Study
notes: Distance MAPE ranged 3.2%-6.1% and error depended on environment activity and watch; brand-level validity flags are insufficient.

title: Review of Validity and Reliability of Garmin Activity Trackers
notes: Validity varied by outcome and model; only four studies assessed reliability and distance energy HR evidence was limited or variable.

title: Evaluating Methods for Imputing Missing Data from Longitudinal Monitoring of Athlete Workload
notes: Context-aware imputation outperformed simple methods in some analyses; imputed derivatives must remain separate from canonical observations.

title: Missing Data in Sport Science: A Didactic Example Using Wearables in American Football
notes: Real-world missingness may violate missing-completely-at-random and bias dashboards; coverage and reasons must be visible.

title: PROV-O: The PROV Ontology
notes: Official W3C standard models entities activities agents derivation attribution revision and time; it is not sport-effect evidence.

title: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio: Conceptual Issues and Fundamental Pitfalls
notes: Identifies ratio artifacts ambiguity and causal overinterpretation; supports descriptive method labeling and prohibits risk or action zones.

title: Mathematical coupling causes spurious correlation within the conventional acute-to-chronic workload ratio calculations
population: Hypothetical 1000-player squad based on elite Australian-football distributions
notes: Coupling produced r=.52 despite independent weeks versus r=.01 when uncoupled; coupling method must be explicit.

title: Does Mathematical Coupling Matter to the Acute to Chronic Workload Ratio? A Case Study From Elite Sport
notes: Counterevidence found mostly trivial coupled-versus-uncoupled effects in two datasets; no coupling variant is universally privileged.

title: The training-injury prevention paradox: should athletes be training smarter and harder?
conflicts: Author-developed ACWR model; exact disclosure not verified
notes: Affirmative ACWR sweet-spot rationale retained as counterevidence history only; zones are not product rules.

title: The Association Between the Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio and Running-Related Injuries in Dutch Runners: A Prospective Cohort Study
notes: Higher ACWR associated inversely with injury and EWMA results were sparse; association is not a causal or product risk rule.

title: Does load management using the acute:chronic workload ratio prevent health problems? A cluster randomised trial of 482 elite youth footballers of both sexes
population: 482 elite Norwegian U19 football players across 34 teams
notes: Tested coupled 7:28 ACWR management and found no health-problem benefit; used only to deny ACWR plan authority.

title: Comparison of Weekly Training Load and Acute: Chronic Workload Ratio Methods to Estimate Change in Training Load in Running
notes: Across 22839 GPS sessions flag rates differed materially by weekly coupled uncoupled and EWMA methods; all calculation choices require provenance.
